questions.text: The determinant of a 1x1 matrix [a] is:
questions.explanation: For a matrix of order 1, the determinant is the element itself.

questions.text: If A is a square matrix of order n, then |kA| is equal to:
questions.explanation: When a scalar k is multiplied by a matrix of order n, each of the n rows is multiplied by k, so the factor k^n comes out of the determinant.

questions.text: A square matrix A is singular if:
questions.explanation: By definition, a singular matrix is a square matrix whose determinant is zero.

questions.text: The area of a triangle with vertices (x1, y1), (x2, y2), and (x3, y3) using determinants is:
questions.explanation: The determinant formula is a compact way to represent the standard coordinate geometry formula for the area of a triangle.

questions.text: If the area of a triangle is 0, then the three vertices are:
questions.explanation: An area of zero implies the points lie on a single straight line.

questions.text: The minor of an element a_ij is denoted by:
questions.explanation: Minor M_ij is the determinant obtained by deleting the i-th row and j-th column.

questions.text: The cofactor A_ij is related to minor M_ij by the formula:
questions.explanation: Cofactors are signed minors, where the sign depends on the sum of row and column indices.

questions.text: For a square matrix A, A(adj A) is equal to:
questions.explanation: A fundamental property of adjoints is that A * adj(A) = adj(A) * A = |A| * I.

questions.text: A square matrix A is invertible if and only if:
questions.explanation: A matrix must have a non-zero determinant (|A| != 0) to have an inverse.

questions.text: If A is an invertible matrix of order 2, then det(A^-1) is equal to:
questions.explanation: Since A * A^-1 = I, taking determinants on both sides gives |A| * |A^-1| = |I| = 1.

A system of equations AX = B is consistent if:
options: It has one or more solutions
questions.explanation: Consistency implies the existence of at least one set of values that satisfies all equations.

questions.text: If |A| != 0, the unique solution for AX = B is given by:
questions.explanation: Multiplying both sides by A^-1 from the left gives A^-1AX = A^-1B, so IX = A^-1B.

questions.text: If |A| = 0 and (adj A)B != 0, then the system has:
questions.explanation: When the determinant is zero and the product of adjoint and B is non-zero, the system is inconsistent.

questions.text: The determinant of a skew-symmetric matrix of odd order is always:
questions.explanation: Property: |A| = |A'| = |-A| = (-1)^n |A|. For odd n, |A| = -|A|, so 2|A|=0.

The sum of products of elements of any row with their own cofactors is equal to:
questions.explanation: This is the definition of expanding a determinant by a row.

questions.text: The sum of products of elements of any row with cofactors of another row is:
questions.explanation: This is a key property of determinants used in matrix inversion proofs.

questions.text: Adjoint of a symmetric matrix is:
questions.explanation: The adjoint operation preserves the symmetry of a matrix.

questions.text: If A is a square matrix, then det(A) = det(A'). This statement is:
questions.explanation: The determinant of a matrix and its transpose are always equal.

questions.text: If any two rows (or columns) of a determinant are identical, its value is:
questions.explanation: Identical rows/columns make the determinant zero (linear dependence).

questions.text: If all elements of a row are zero, the determinant is:
questions.explanation: Expanding along the zero row yields 0.

questions.text: For two square matrices A and B of the same order, |AB| is equal to:
questions.explanation: The determinant of a product is the product of the determinants.

questions.text: If A is a scalar matrix of order n with diagonal elements k, then |A| is:
questions.explanation: For a diagonal matrix, the determinant is the product of diagonal elements.

questions.text: A system of linear equations is called homogeneous if B is:
questions.explanation: Homogeneous systems are of the form AX = 0.

questions.text: The determinant of an identity matrix of any order is:
questions.explanation: Product of diagonal elements (1 * 1 * ...) is always 1.

options: Determinant is a number associated to a matrix
Determinant is a number associated to a square matrix
questions.explanation: Determinants are defined only for square matrices.

questions.text: If A is an invertible matrix, then adj(A) is:
questions.explanation: Since A^-1 = (1/|A|) adj A, then adj A = |A| A^-1.

questions.text: The number of solutions of a system AX = B where |A| != 0 is:
questions.explanation: Non-singular matrices always provide a unique solution for the system.

questions.text: If x, y, z are non-zero real numbers, then inverse of [[x,0,0],[0,y,0],[0,0,z]] is:
questions.explanation: The inverse of a diagonal matrix is a diagonal matrix with the reciprocal of its elements.

questions.text: The determinant of a diagonal matrix is:
questions.explanation: Expansion along any row/column reduces to the product of diagonals.

questions.text: The value of det [[1, a, b+c], [1, b, c+a], [1, c, a+b]] is:
questions.explanation: C3 -> C3 + C2 makes C3 = [a+b+c, a+b+c, a+b+c]. Taking common, C1 and C3 become identical.

questions.explanation: Inverse exists if |A| != 0.

questions.explanation: The inverse of product is the product of inverses in reverse order.
